commentary:
ٱلْقَارِعَةُ

The surah opens with a single, striking word: Al-Qari'ah. Among the many names the Quran gives to Judgment Day - Al-Qiyamah, As-Sa'ah, At-Tammah Al-Kubra - Al-Qari'ah appears five times in the Quran, three of them in this very surah.

The word القارعة derives from a root meaning several interrelated concepts. Originally, it describes something that has been beaten down until it becomes level and barren - like a bald head where hair once grew, or a courtyard stripped bare of vegetation. The Arabs used qariya for the highest, hardest part of a road, or for a well when its water has run out and you can see the rocky bottom.

But there's another crucial dimension to this word. When something rigid and barren is struck, it produces a violent, echoing sound. Picture a stick hitting exposed, rocky earth - that harsh, reverberating sound is also called qar'. By extension, the Arabs used this word figuratively for being beaten down by harsh words, for taking a verbal beating so severe it feels physical. The scholars suggest that Judgment Day is called Al-Qari'ah both because it will leave the earth utterly barren and because it will deliver a beating to the enemies of Allah ﷻ - not just physical, but through the terrifying scenes people will witness.

This single word captures everything at once: the loud, striking sound; the barren landscape left in its wake; the beating and terror it brings. It's as though Allah ﷻ is announcing: "The Strike."

مَا ٱلْقَارِعَةُ

When someone utters a single, terrifying word - just "The Strike" or "Fire!" - what's the natural human reaction? Questions immediately arise: "What strike? What are you talking about? Who's being struck? What happened?"

Allah ﷻ captures this natural human response: مَا الْقَارِعَةُ - "What is Al-Qari'ah?" This isn't Allah ﷻ asking Himself a question. This is Allah ﷻ voicing the question that naturally arises in the listener's mind. When you hear this terrifying word, you immediately want to know more.

By presenting this as a question, Allah ﷻ creates anticipation. You're expecting an answer. You're waiting to understand what this catastrophic event could possibly be.

translation: And what can make you know what is the Striking Calamity
commentary:
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْقَارِعَةُ

But instead of a straightforward answer, Allah ﷻ intensifies the mystery: وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا الْقَارِعَةُ - "And what could make you know what Al-Qari'ah is?"

This is a profound rhetorical statement. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: "You have absolutely no means of knowing. There's no way for you to comprehend this. This warning cannot be prepared for through your own understanding."

Consider the contrast with the previous surah, Al-Adiyat. There, Allah ﷻ described the morning raid - the enemy could be seen coming. The sun had risen, dust clouds appeared on the horizon. There were warning signs, however terrifying. But now comes a different kind of raid, one that starts with a deafening sound with absolutely no warning. You have no clue when it's coming, no clue how devastating it will be. Only Allah ﷻ knows.

This connects beautifully to another surah with similar rhetoric: Al-Haqqah (69). That surah opens with الْحَاقَّةُ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ - "The Reality! What is the Reality? And what could make you know what the Reality is?" The parallel is deliberate. Al-Haqqah means "The Reality," and immediately after mentioning it, that surah references Al-Qari'ah: كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ وَعَادٌ بِالْقَارِعَةِ.

There's a deep connection here between reality (haqq) and the striking sound (qari'ah). Right now, in this life, there are sounds that only Allah ﷻ produces - His words, His revelation. If you can internalize the revelation of Allah ﷻ, you can appreciate reality as it truly is. You can reach Al-Haqqah through these words. But if His words won't work for you, if they won't shake you into awareness now, then the only sound that will finally get through to you is Al-Qari'ah.

It's like the old saying: some people can't learn by words alone - they only learn through hard experience. Unless reality hits them like a blow, they won't get it. This is the subtle warning embedded in these opening ayahs.

translation: It is the Day when people will be like moths, dispersed
commentary:
يَوْمَ يَكُونُ ٱلنَّاسُ كَٱلْفَرَاشِ ٱلْمَبْثُوثِ

Now Allah ﷻ gives us two brief, powerful images of what happens when that sound strikes. He doesn't give us a complete picture - just two snapshots of the reaction to Al-Qari'ah.

يَوْمَ يَكُونُ النَّاسُ كَالْفَرَاشِ الْمَبْثُوثِ - "The Day when people will be like scattered moths."

The common translation - "scattered moths" - captures one layer of meaning, but there's more depth here. Moths emerge when the earth shakes. They're easily attracted to flame. They scatter in every direction despite their huge numbers, with no sense of direction, often just following whatever moth is in front of them. Allah ﷻ is describing humanity in utter chaos, perhaps even rushing toward fire.

But farash has other meanings that deepen this image. It can mean something delicate that spreads out - light leaves from a sapling that scatter when the wind blows. It can refer to land that slides easily, where pebbles keep moving and nothing settles.

Most strikingly, farash was used for the small chips of bone that remain when a skull has cracked and fractured - the fragments scattered about from someone who died in the desert. This becomes significant when we reach the end of the surah.

The word mabthuth (scattered) adds another dimension. The root word ba-tha means to disperse something that was once contained or held together - like sand held in a clump that suddenly spreads, or horses standing together then unleashed to charge in all directions. News spreading uncontrollably is also described with this word.

Remarkably, ba-tha also describes a particular state of overwhelming sadness - when someone is so grief-stricken that their mind scatters, unable to concentrate on anything, constantly pulled in different directions by their distress. Judgment Day is described elsewhere as يَوْمَ الْحَسْرَةِ - a day of extreme regret and grief.

So كَالْفَرَاشِ الْمَبْثُوثِ captures not just scattered moths, but moths overwhelmed with grief - people experiencing both terror and profound sadness simultaneously. When that sound hits, Allah ﷻ is telling us, people won't just be fearful; they'll be consumed by devastating sorrow.

translation: And the mountains will be like wool, fluffed up
commentary:
وَتَكُونُ ٱلْجِبَالُ كَٱلْعِهْنِ ٱلْمَنفُوشِ

After describing people, Allah ﷻ turns to mountains: وَتَكُونُ الْجِبَالُ كَالْعِهْنِ الْمَنفُوشِ - "And the mountains will be like wool, fluffed up."

The word 'ihn refers to something that appears solid from a distance but, when you actually touch it, proves soft, weak, and crumbling. It looks firm but disperses at the slightest shake - it has no real substance.

The Quran gives various descriptions of mountains on Judgment Day - being crushed, moving, scattered. But this might be the most graphic: mountains as soft as wool. Not just any wool, but manfush - torn, fluffed wool. The word nafsaha means to tear apart, to scatter possessions, to pull wool into tiny flakes that float weightlessly in the air.

First, Allah ﷻ described people as weightless as moths or leaves. Now He describes mountains as equally weightless - mere flakes of wool floating away.

This parallel is crucial. From a material standpoint, nothing is heavier than mountains. From a social standpoint, nothing carries more weight than the power and might of nations. Civilizations throughout history have compared their strength to mountains - symbols of permanence, stability, unmovable power.

But look at what Allah ﷻ does here. He takes what was once a symbol of human strength - people, nations, massive populations - and reduces them to the insignificance of scattered moths. Then He takes the very material symbol of strength - mountains themselves - and strips them of all weight and value.

Things that used to carry weight in this life no longer have weight. Things that were heavy have become utterly light and insignificant. The reality of this world has fundamentally changed. If the old measures of value have been reversed, then something else must have become significant. What used to be weightless must have become weighty.

## Ayah 6-7

فَأَمَّا مَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ
فَهُوَ فِى عِيشَةٍ رَّاضِيَةٍ

With this understanding, read these ayahs: فَأَمَّا مَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَازِينُهُ فَهُوَ فِي عِيشَةٍ رَاضِيَةٍ - "Then as for one whose scales are heavy, he will be in a pleasant life."

The word mawazin can mean either "scales" (the instruments of weighing) or "things being weighed." Both translations work: as for the one whose scales become heavy, or as for the one whose items being weighed turn out to be heavy.

Before we examine this further, consider what happened in the preceding ayahs. In this life, we value things in only two ways: material weight (gold, property, physical goods measured on scales) or social weight (degrees, status, reputation - the value people assign to things). We've become accustomed to measuring our own worth based on either material possessions or social standing - how we dress, what we drive, how we compare to others, whether we fit in with certain standards.

We impose these weights on ourselves and become consumed by them. People obsess over material goods, brands, expensive items whose value comes not from their utility but from perception. A bag might cost thousands of dollars not because of the materials or craftsmanship, but because of the brand label - a perceived value that makes someone feel they have worth when others notice it.

But in these ayahs, Allah ﷻ eliminated both systems of weight. People themselves became weightless. Mountains became weightless. Material goods have no value. Social standing has no meaning. Both scales - the material and the social - have been completely dismantled.

Now, what actually carries weight? Your deeds, and specifically, your deeds combined with the sincerity behind them.

Think about what we do in this life. You might say something to someone that you consider completely insignificant - just words, just a text message you typed and sent without thinking. It carries no weight in your life right now. But on Judgment Day, those words could be among the heaviest things in your scale.

The Prophet ﷺ told us that phrases we might say without thinking - SubhanAllah, Alhamdulillah, La ilaha illAllah - are weighty words in Allah's sight. Someone might ask "How are you?" and you respond "Alhamdulillah" without even thinking about it. You didn't consider those words significant. But Allah ﷻ may make those words massive on Judgment Day.

Similarly, we underestimate both our good and bad deeds. The Prophet ﷺ told us of someone who simply gave water to a thirsty dog and entered Paradise. Did that person think, "This will get me into Paradise"? No, they just acted on natural mercy and moved on with their life. You might make a crying child smile, then forget about it completely. That could be the heaviest deed you ever did.

This is why sincerity matters so profoundly, but we need to understand sincerity correctly. We've complicated this concept. Sincerity (ikhlas) isn't only toward Allah ﷻ - it's also toward people.

In acts of worship - prayer, charity, learning Quran, teaching Quran - sincerity must be exclusively for Allah ﷻ. Whether one person is watching or a thousand, whether the camera is on or off, whether you're given the prime speaking slot or the morning session - none of that should change your motivation. If it does, the deed is no longer purely for Allah ﷻ.

But then there are deeds where sincerity is toward people: taking care of your family, being good to your parents, honest with your employer, sincere to your clients, kind to a friend. That's also sincerity, and it will also be heavy on Judgment Day. The hearts won't just be tested for what we did for Allah ﷻ, but also for what we did for and toward people.

When that man gave water to the dog, was he thinking, "Let me purify my intention before helping this animal"? No, he felt sincere mercy toward a suffering creature. That sincerity toward creation also counts.

فَأَمَّا مَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَازِينُهُ - as for the one whose deeds actually carry weight. Where does that weight come from? The deed was good, and the intention behind it was sincere - whether toward Allah ﷻ or toward creation.

فَهُوَ فِي عِيشَةٍ رَاضِيَةٍ - "He will be in a pleasant life."

The verb tense here is remarkable. Allah ﷻ could have said "he will be" using a future form. Instead, He uses a form that implies such a person is already in a good life. Right now, no matter how miserable their circumstances might appear, they're already living well. They don't even know it.

The Prophet ﷺ and his companions were being tortured, mocked, ridiculed - the most oppressed people in their society. How were they living in a good life? But they were, because 'isha means to live with provision and comfort, and radiya means contentment and satisfaction.

Living in luxury doesn't necessarily make you content. But Allah ﷻ combines both: عِيشَةٍ رَاضِيَةٍ - a life that's both comfortable and makes you content. A life where you find tranquility, where you're at peace with yourself, where you're not in constant turmoil.

Where does our turmoil come from? From comparing ourselves to those who have more, from measuring ourselves against the standards people have created, from constantly feeling we don't have what others have. But when you find a way to make your scales heavy through meaningful deeds, you're already living in contentment.

By contrast, those not focused on piling up good deeds are like scattered moths (كَالْفَرَاشِ الْمَبْثُوثِ) - running in every direction, filled with grief, never satisfied, never at peace.

## Ayah 8-9

وَأَمَّا مَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ
فَأُمُّهُۥ هَاوِيَةٌ

وَأَمَّا مَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوَازِينُهُ - "But as for one whose scales are light..."

The word khaffat comes from khuff, meaning light or soft. It's used for an animal's paw because it's soft and, when pressed down, spreads the weight so it feels lighter. It's used for soft leather socks. Figuratively, it describes someone who's lost their mind or become reckless. When Pharaoh manipulated his people, the Quran says فَاسْتَخَفَّ قَوْمَهُ - he made his people's minds light, he dumbed them down through propaganda, made them think lightly of themselves and act recklessly.

So خَفَّتْ مَوَازِينُهُ suggests not only that the deeds are light, but that they must be light because the person lived a reckless, careless life. They didn't take their choices seriously.

Now notice: this ayah doesn't say, "As for the one whose bad deeds are heavy, he's going to Hell." It simply says, "As for the one whose good deeds are too light..." The implication is devastating - you might spend your whole life doing things that seem meaningful by worldly standards, but gathering nothing of real significance for the Hereafter. You don't even need an excess of bad deeds to face consequences; a deficit of meaningful good is enough.

فَأُمُّهُ هَاوِيَةٌ - "His mother is Hawiyah."

The word umm means mother, but it also means the essence of something, or a gathering place. A mother is called umm because she gathers the flesh of her child inside her belly, nurturing it gently as it grows. The front of the skull protecting the brain is called umm ar-ra's - the mother of the head.

Hawiyah comes from hawa, meaning to fall into a deep place. 

There are three interpretations of this phrase, and all three work together to create a complete picture:

First, Hawiyah is one of the names of Hellfire - a deep fire that throws people down into its depths. It's as though Allah ﷻ is saying: just as your earthly mother gathered you, embraced you, protected you, gave you refuge when you were afraid - now you have a new mother. But this mother doesn't embrace with love. This is a mother of spite and ruthlessness, the complete opposite of what a mother should be. 

Hell, some scholars note, is terrifying precisely because it represents what remains when you remove Allah's mercy from the equation. A mother's defining quality is rahmah (mercy), which comes from the same root as rahim (womb). Without Allah's rahmah, you're left with an umm without rahmah - a mother of fire instead of love.

Second, remember I mentioned that umm ar-ra's means the front of the skull, and farash can mean skull fragments? فَأُمُّهُ هَاوِيَةٌ could mean: his forehead will be the first thing to fall in. He's diving into Hell head first. Not hesitating, not being pushed feet first - but checking in himself, head first, skull cracking.

Third, and most convincing based on pre-Islamic Arabic usage: the Arabs had expressions for wishing destruction on someone, and one of them was hawiyat ummuhu - "may his mother collapse." They weren't insulting the mother; they were expressing how devastating the calamity would be. It meant: "May something so terrible happen to you that when your mother hears about it, she collapses from grief."

This poetic phrase captures something profound. In worldly terms, this person might have everything others want - success, wealth, status. The only thing they're bankrupt of is deeds with real value. Everyone else might be celebrating their worldly success. Their mother might be proud and happy. But Allah ﷻ is saying: your mother has no reason to be happy. You've destroyed yourself eternally. She should feel like she's attending your funeral. That's how she should feel.

## Ayah 10-11

وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا هِيَهْ
نَارٌ حَامِيَةٌۢ

وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا هِيَهْ - "And what could make you know what that is?"

The surah opened with a question: مَا الْقَارِعَةُ - "What is Al-Qari'ah?" followed by وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا الْقَارِعَةُ - "What could make you know what Al-Qari'ah is?" Answer first, then question.

Now at the end, the pattern reverses: question first - وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا هِيَهْ - followed by the answer: نَارٌ حَامِيَةٌ - "A raging Fire."

The word hamiyah means intensely hot, like the blazing heat of the sun at its peak. But hama in Arabic also means "to prevent." A doctor who prevents his patient from eating harmful food is doing himaya - prevention, protection. 

This is devastating wordplay. A mother's job is to prevent harm from reaching you, to protect you from outside dangers. This new "mother" is also protective - but it's made of fire, and it prevents anything good from ever reaching you. It's a wall of fire that keeps all good away.

Fire has another quality: whatever has value, when thrown into fire, becomes valueless. Money, houses, cars - fire came and their value is gone. Your deeds had no value, described like scattered dust (هَبَاءً مَنثُورًا). If the deeds have no value, it's as though they burned to nothing. And so did you.

نَارٌ حَامِيَةٌ - a Fire that rages and prevents, that destroys value and blocks all good eternally.

## The Structure and Connection

The surah's structure is elegant and symmetrical:

**Opening**: الْقَارِعَةُ مَا الْقَارِعَةُ وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا الْقَارِعَةُ (answer, question, rhetorical question)

**Closing**: فَأُمُّهُ هَاوِيَةٌ وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا هِيَهْ نَارٌ حَامِيَةٌ (answer, rhetorical question, answer)

**Middle section**: Two images of weightlessness (people and mountains), then two outcomes based on weight (heavy scales and light scales)

This surah completes Surah Az-Zilzal (99). That surah described the earthquake - إِذَا زُلْزِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ زِلْزَالَهَا. Now we learn what causes that earthquake: Al-Qari'ah, the striking sound.

Az-Zilzal said people would come out scattered: يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصْدُرُ النَّاسُ أَشْتَاتًا. Al-Qari'ah shows us what that looks like: كَالْفَرَاشِ الْمَبْثُوثِ - like scattered moths.

Az-Zilzal said people would be shown their deeds: لِيُرَوْا أَعْمَالَهُمْ. But seeing isn't enough - the deeds must be weighed. Al-Qari'ah addresses the weighing: ثَقُلَتْ مَوَازِينُهُ and خَفَّتْ مَوَازِينُهُ.

Az-Zilzal mentioned that even a speck's weight of good or evil would be seen: فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْرًا يَرَهُ. Al-Qari'ah builds on this: maybe that speck will turn out to be incredibly heavy - ثَقُلَتْ مَوَازِينُهُ.

Most remarkably, Az-Zilzal said the earth would bring forth its burdens: وَأَخْرَجَتِ الْأَرْضُ أَثْقَالَهَا. Mountains are no longer burdens - they're weightless. What is the earth actually burdened with? The hearts and souls of humanity. Those are what truly have weight.

In Az-Zilzal, people ask: مَا لَهَا - "What is wrong with it?" But in Al-Qari'ah, when the sound becomes deafening, even their voices are silenced. You can't hear people anymore over the overwhelming sound of Al-Qari'ah.

Finally, Az-Zilzal mentioned good deeds and bad deeds. Al-Qari'ah mentions good deeds and worthless deeds. Together, all three categories are covered: the good, the bad, and the worthless. The picture is complete.

May Allah ﷻ make our scales heavy with deeds done sincerely for His sake and with excellence toward His creation. May He protect us from Al-Qari'ah and grant us from عِيشَةٍ رَاضِيَةٍ in this life and the next.

translation: Then as for one whose scales are heavy [with good deeds]
He will be in a pleasant life
But as for one whose scales are light
His refuge will be an abyss
And what can make you know what that is
It is a Fire, intensely hot
